questions.question:
Horizontal microprogramming:
A. does not require use of signal decoders
B. results in larger sized microinstructions than vertical microprogramming
C. uses one bit for each control signal
D. all of the above
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) correctly describe horizontal microprogramming

understand.plain: Microprogramming is a method of implementing a processor's control unit using a sequence of micro-operations stored in a control memory. In horizontal microprogramming, each bit in a microinstruction directly controls one hardware signal, so no decoding is needed. This leads to wider (larger) microinstructions compared to vertical microprogramming, which encodes multiple signals into fewer bits and requires decoders.

keywords.explain: Each control signal gets its own dedicated bit in the microinstruction word, with no encoding/decoding required.
keywords.example: If there are 64 control signals, a horizontal microinstruction has 64 bits, one per signal.

keywords.explain: Control signals are encoded into a compact field; decoders expand them into actual control lines at runtime.
keywords.example: 64 signals can be encoded in 6 bits (log_2(64) = 6), needing a 6-to-64 decoder.

keywords.explain: A hardware circuit that takes an encoded binary field and activates one of 2^n output lines.
keywords.example: A 3-to-8 decoder takes a 3-bit field and enables one of 8 control lines.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220' viewBox='0 0 520 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='230' height='90' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='125' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Horizontal</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='25' x='122' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s4</text><rect x='140' y='50' width='25' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='152' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s5</text><rect x='170' y='50' width='25' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='182' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s6</text><rect x='200' y='50' width='25' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='212' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s7</text><text x='125' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 bit per signal, no decoder, wider word</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='230' height='90' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='395' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Vertical</text><rect x='290' y='50' width='55' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='317' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3-bit code</text><rect x='350' y='50' width='55' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='377' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3-bit code</text><text x='395' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Encoded; needs decoder; narrower word</text><text x='260' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>vs</text><text x='125' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Horizontal: wider, 1 bit/signal, no decoder</text><text x='395' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Vertical: narrower, encoded, needs decoder</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Comparison of horizontal microprogramming (one bit per signal, wide microinstruction, no decoder) versus vertical microprogramming (encoded fields, narrow microinstruction, needs decoder).

given.aim: Identify which statements about horizontal microprogramming are correct.

terms.meaning: Each bit of a microinstruction directly maps to one control signal
terms.connects: This is the definition; all three statements (A, B, C) follow directly from it

terms.meaning: Because each bit directly drives one signal, no decoding logic is needed
terms.example: Bit 5 = 1 means signal 5 is activated, no intermediate decode step

terms.meaning: Horizontal words are as wide as the number of control signals, unlike vertical where signals are encoded
terms.example: 64 signals: horizontal = 64 bits, vertical ~= 6 bits per group

given.plan: Evaluate each statement (A, B, C) against the definition of horizontal microprogramming. Since all three are defining characteristics, the answer is D (all of the above).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A: does not require signal decoders
steps.apply: In horizontal microprogramming, each microinstruction bit is directly connected to one control signal. There is no encoding, so no decoder is needed. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: Vertical microprogramming encodes signals and needs decoders to expand them.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B: results in larger microinstructions than vertical
steps.formula_raw: microinstruction width (horizontal) = number of control signals
steps.apply: Horizontal uses 1 bit per signal. Vertical encodes multiple signals into fewer bits. So horizontal microinstructions are wider. Statement B is TRUE.
steps.note: More parallelism possible in horizontal since multiple signals can be set simultaneously with no group constraints.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C: uses one bit for each control signal
steps.apply: This is the fundamental property of horizontal microprogramming by definition. Statement C is TRUE.
steps.note: This is what distinguishes horizontal from vertical microprogramming.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: All three statements A, B, and C are correct. Therefore the answer is D: all of the above.
steps.note: A common GATE trap: students think only one option is correct, but here all three are defining properties.

questions.formula_note: Horizontal microprogramming: 1 bit per control signal, no decoder required, widest microinstructions; vertical microprogramming: encoded fields, needs decoders, narrower microinstructions.

questions.question:
The microinstructions stored in the control memory of a processor have a width of 36 bits. Each microinstruction is divided into three fields: a micro-operation field of 23 bits, a next address field (X), and a MUX select field (Y). There are 8 status bits in the input of the MUX. How many bits are there in X and Y, and what is the size of the control memory in number of words?
A. 10, 3, 1024
B. 8, 5, 256
C. 5, 8, 2048
D. 10, 3, 512
questions.to_find: Number of bits in next-address field X and MUX-select field Y, and the size of control memory in words

understand.plain: A microinstruction is 36 bits wide and split into three parts: a 23-bit micro-operation field, a next-address field X (used to select the next microinstruction address), and a MUX select field Y (used to choose among status inputs). The MUX has 8 status lines, so Y must be large enough to select one of them. The remaining bits after 23 and Y go to X, which determines the size of the control memory.

keywords.explain: The field in a microinstruction that specifies the address of the next microinstruction to execute.
keywords.example: If X = 10 bits, the control memory can hold 2^10 = 1024 microinstructions.

keywords.explain: The field that selects which status bit (condition) will be used to decide the next address via a multiplexer.
keywords.example: With 8 status inputs, Y needs ceil(log_2(8)) = 3 bits to select one of them.

keywords.explain: The number of microinstruction words storable, determined by 2^X where X is the width of the next-address field.
keywords.example: X = 10 bits -> 2^10 = 1024 words of control memory.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='130' viewBox='0 0 520 130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='280' height='50' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='150' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Micro-operation field</text><text x='150' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>23 bits</text><rect x='295' y='20' width='115' height='50' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='352' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Next addr (X)</text><text x='352' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>10 bits</text><rect x='415' y='20' width='95' height='50' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='462' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>MUX sel (Y)</text><text x='462' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 bits</text><text x='260' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Total = 23 + 10 + 3 = 36 bits | Control memory = 2^10 = 1024 words</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 36-bit microinstruction divided into: 23-bit micro-operation field, 10-bit next address field (X), and 3-bit MUX select field (Y). Control memory size = 2^10 = 1024 words.

given.aim: Find the bit widths of X (next address) and Y (MUX select), and the control memory size.

terms.example: micro-op (23) + X + Y = 36, so X + Y = 13
terms.connects: Constrains the values of X and Y

terms.meaning: 8 status bits are inputs to the MUX
terms.example: To select 1 of 8 inputs, need ceil(log_2(8)) = 3 bits for Y

given.plan: First find Y from the number of MUX inputs (Y = ceil(log_2(8)) = 3). Then find X = 36 - 23 - 3 = 10. Control memory size = 2^X = 2^10 = 1024 words.

steps.title: Find MUX select field width Y
steps.apply: There are 8 status bits as inputs to the MUX. To select one of 8 inputs: Y = ceil(log_2(8)) = ceil(3) = 3 bits.
steps.note: A MUX with n inputs needs ceil(log_2(n)) select lines.

steps.title: Find next address field width X
steps.formula_raw: X = Total width - micro-op field - Y
steps.note: The three fields must sum to 36 bits exactly.

steps.title: Find control memory size
steps.note: The next address field determines how many distinct microinstruction addresses can be referenced.

steps.title: Match to answer options
steps.apply: X = 10, Y = 3, control memory = 1024 words -> matches option A: 10, 3, 1024.

questions.formula_note: MUX select bits Y = ceil(log_2(#status_inputs)); next-address field X = total_width - op_field - Y; control memory size = 2^X words.

questions.question:
Consider the following sequence of micro-operations.
MBR <- PC
MAR <- X
PC <- Y
Memory <- MBR

Which one of the following is a possible operation performed by this sequence?
A. Instruction fetch
B. Operand fetch
C. Conditional branch
D. Initiation of interrupt service
questions.to_find: Which CPU operation is performed by the given sequence of micro-operations

understand.plain: The sequence stores the Program Counter (PC) into memory at address X, and sets PC to Y. This pattern — saving the current PC value to a fixed memory location and jumping to a new address — is exactly what happens at the start of an interrupt service routine: the return address (old PC) is saved and the processor jumps to the interrupt handler at address Y.

keywords.explain: Holds data being read from or written to memory.
keywords.example: MBR <- PC stores the current program counter value in MBR before writing to memory.

keywords.explain: Holds the address of the memory location to be accessed.
keywords.example: MAR <- X means we will access memory location X.

keywords.explain: When an interrupt occurs, the processor saves the current PC (return address) to memory and jumps to the interrupt handler address.
keywords.example: Save PC at address X, load interrupt handler address Y into PC.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='200' viewBox='0 0 500 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='100' height='40' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='60' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>PC</text><rect x='200' y='20' width='100' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='250' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>MBR</text><line x1='110' y1='40' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='155' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>MBR<-PC</text><rect x='200' y='100' width='100' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='250' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Memory[X]</text><line x1='250' y1='60' x2='250' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='270' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Mem<-MBR</text><rect x='10' y='140' width='100' height='40' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='60' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff'>PC = Y</text><text x='155' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Jump to handler</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='250' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Save return addr at X, jump to interrupt handler Y</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Micro-operation sequence: PC saved to MBR, MBR written to memory at address X (saving return address), PC set to Y (interrupt handler address). This initiates interrupt service.

given.aim: Identify which processor operation is implemented by the four micro-operations.

terms.meaning: Copy the current program counter value into the memory buffer register
terms.example: Preserves return address before modifying PC
terms.connects: Prepares the return address for saving to memory

terms.meaning: Load address X into the memory address register
terms.example: X is a fixed or designated memory location for saving state
terms.connects: Points to where the return address will be stored

terms.meaning: Load address Y into PC, redirecting execution
terms.example: Y is the interrupt handler's starting address
terms.connects: Causes CPU to jump to interrupt service routine

given.plan: Trace the effect of each micro-operation. The PC is saved to memory, then PC is changed to Y. This is the classic interrupt initiation pattern: save return address, jump to handler. Eliminate other options by reasoning about what each one requires.

steps.title: Trace the micro-operation sequence
steps.apply: Step 1: MBR <- PC (copy current PC to MBR). Step 2: MAR <- X (set memory address to X). Step 3: PC <- Y (jump to address Y). Step 4: Memory <- MBR (write old PC to Memory[X]).
steps.note: The net effect: old PC is saved at memory address X, and PC is now Y.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A (Instruction fetch): fetches from Memory[PC] to IR, does not save PC to memory. B (Operand fetch): fetches operand from memory, does not change PC. C (Conditional branch): would only change PC conditionally based on a flag, and does not save PC to memory.
steps.note: None of A, B, C involve saving PC to memory AND setting PC to a new address.

steps.title: Match to interrupt service initiation
steps.apply: Interrupt initiation requires: (1) save current PC (return address) to a known memory location, (2) load interrupt handler address into PC. The sequence does exactly this: saves PC at address X, jumps to Y. Answer is D.
steps.note: In real processors, more registers (like PSW) may also be saved, but saving PC and jumping to handler is the minimal model shown.

questions.formula_note: Interrupt service initiation pattern: save current PC to memory (return address), load interrupt handler base address into PC. Matches classic interrupt acknowledgment micro-operation sequence.

questions.question:
A CPU has only three instructions I1, I2 and I3, which use the following signals in time steps T1-T5:
I1: T1: Ain, Bout, Cin  T2: PCout, Bin  T3: (T1+T2).T3 = T1,T1  T4: Bin, Cout  T5: End
I2: T1: Aout, Bin  T2: PCout, Bin  T3: Din, Aout  T4: Bin, Cout  T5: End
I3: T1: Din, Aout  T2: PCout, Bin  T3: Din, Aout  T4: Zin, Aout  T5: End

Which of the following pairs of expressions represent the circuit for generating control signals S_30 and S_40 respectively?
A. S_30 = T3 + I1*T3 and S_40 = T1*(I1+I2)*T4
B. S_30 = (I1+I2)*T3 and S_40 = T1*(I1+I2)*T4
C. S_30 = T1 + I2*T3 and S_40 = T1*(I1+I2)*T4
D. S_30 = T1 + (I2+I3)*T3 and S_40 = (I1+I2)*T4
questions.to_find: Boolean expressions for hardwired control signals S_30 and S_40 generated from the instruction-time-step table

understand.plain: In a hardwired control unit, each control signal is asserted (set to 1) based on which instruction is executing and which time step the processor is in. To find the expression for a control signal, you look at every combination of (instruction, time-step) that requires that signal, and OR those terms together. The image shows a table where rows are instructions (I1, I2, I3) and columns are time steps (T1-T5), with signals listed in each cell.

keywords.explain: A signal whose Boolean expression is derived directly from the instruction decoder and timing circuitry, without a control memory.
keywords.example: Signal S is active when (I1 AND T2) OR (I3 AND T4) etc.

keywords.explain: The clock cycle within an instruction's execution. Each instruction takes several steps.
keywords.example: T1 is the first cycle; the signal decoder checks both the current instruction and the current time step.

keywords.explain: A Boolean sum-of-products (SOP) expression covering all (instruction, time-step) pairs that assert the signal.
keywords.example: If signal X is needed in I1,T2 and I2,T3: X = I1*T2 + I2*T3.

class='sub mut'>S30/40</text><rect x='150' y='80' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>PCout,Bin</text><rect x='230' y='80' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Din,Aout</text><rect x='310' y='80' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='350' y='100' text-anchor='middle' ink'>Din,Aout</text><rect x='150' y='120' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>PCout,Bin</text><rect x='230' y='120' width='80' height='40' ink'>Zin,Aout</text><rect x='390' y='120' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='430' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>End</text><text x='260' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Highlighted cells show where S30/S40 are asserted</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing signals for instructions I1, I2, I3 across time steps T1-T5. S30 is asserted in (I1,T1), (I2,T1), (I3,T1), (I1,T3), (I2,T3), (I3,T3). S40 is asserted in (I1,T4) and (I2,T4).

given.aim: Derive Boolean expressions for control signals S30 and S40 from the given instruction-timing table.

terms.meaning: A hardwired control signal active during certain (instruction, time-step) pairs
terms.example: S30 is Aout/Bout type signal active in T1 for all instructions and T3 for I1, I2, I3
terms.connects: Reading from the table which cells assert S30 gives the SOP expression

terms.meaning: Another control signal active in specific instruction-time-step combinations
terms.example: S40 (Bin or Cout type) active in T4 for I1 and I2 only, not I3

terms.meaning: Boolean expression form: OR of AND-terms, one per asserted (instruction, time-step) pair
terms.example: Signal active in (I1,T2) and (I3,T4) -> expression: I1*T2 + I3*T4
terms.connects: Used to build each control signal's logic circuit

given.plan: From the table, identify all (instruction, time-step) pairs where S30 and S40 are asserted. Form SOP expressions. Check which answer option matches.

steps.title: Read the table for S30 (Aout or Bout signal at T1 and T3)
steps.formula_raw: S = OR of all (I_i * T_j) where signal is active
steps.apply: From the image: S30 (likely Aout or related) is asserted in: I1 at T1 (Ain,Bout,Cin includes Bout), I2 at T1 (Aout,Bin includes Aout), I3 at T1 (Din,Aout includes Aout). Also at T3: I1 (has some signal), I2 (Din,Aout), I3 (Din,Aout). So S30 = (I1+I2+I3)*T1 + (I2+I3)*T3 = T1 + (I2+I3)*T3 (since I1+I2+I3 at T1 = T1 for all instructions).
steps.note: When a signal is asserted for ALL instructions at a given time step, the instruction selector drops out: (I1+I2+I3)*T_n = T_n.

steps.title: Read the table for S40 (Bin or Cout signal at T4)
steps.formula_raw: S40 = sum of (I_i * T4) for each I that asserts S40 at T4
steps.apply: S40 (likely Bin or Cout) is asserted at T4 for: I1 (Bin,Cout) and I2 (Bin,Cout). I3 at T4 has Zin,Aout — NOT Bin or Cout. So S40 = I1*T4 + I2*T4 = (I1+I2)*T4.
steps.note: I3 does not assert S40 at T4, so I3 is not included in the expression.

steps.title: Match expressions to answer options
steps.apply: We derived: S30 = T1 + (I2+I3)*T3 and S40 = (I1+I2)*T4. This matches option D exactly.
steps.note: Option A includes I1*T3 in S30 but not (I2+I3)*T3 which is incomplete. Option B has (I1+I2)*T3 missing I3*T3. Option C has T1 + I2*T3 missing I3*T3. Only D is correct.

questions.formula_note: Hardwired control signal derivation: for each signal, OR together all (instruction AND time-step) product terms where that signal is asserted; simplify by factoring when a signal is asserted for all instructions at a given step.
